metadata.title: The Photoelectric Effect
metadata.notes: Pragmatist-register variant of vox-photoelectric-effect. Humanitarians palette. Voice: am_onyx. Includes CLI exercise beat.
chapters: Try it: simulate the photoelectric effect
metadata.description_blurb: The photoelectric effect: what it is, what the equation predicts, where the wave model fails, and a runnable Claude exercise to simulate it.

beats.narration_text: The photoelectric effect: UV light hits metal and ejects electrons. Three measurable behaviors that the wave model predicts incorrectly. One equation that gets all three right. Here is what you need to know.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The photoelectric effect: UV light hits metal and ejects electrons. Three measurable behaviors that the wave model predicts incorrectly. One equation that gets all three right. Here is what you need to know.

props.command: Explain the photoelectric effect: what it measures, the three failure modes of the wave model, Einstein's photon hypothesis, and the equation K_max = h*nu - Phi.
output: threshold frequency — wave model predicts: none
KE independent of intensity — wave model predicts: proportional
instant emission — wave model predicts: time delay

beats.narration_text: In 1887, Heinrich Hertz confirmed light is a wave — the dominant model of classical physics. That same year, he observed an anomaly that would eventually require abandoning it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: In 1887, Heinrich Hertz confirmed light is a wave — the dominant model of classical physics. That same year, he observed an anomaly that would eventually require abandoning it.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Hertz (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Heinrich Hertz portrait or spark-gap apparatus, 1880s; set shot.focus on his face / the gap

beats.narration_text: The setup: UV light strikes a metal surface. Electrons are ejected. Three features of those electrons contradict every prediction the wave model makes.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The setup: UV light strikes a metal surface. Electrons are ejected. Three features of those electrons contradict every prediction the wave model makes.

card.title: THE PHOTOELECTRIC EFFECT
card.sub: three failures of the wave model

beats.narration_text: By 1902, Philipp Lenard had identified the ejected particles as electrons. By 1914, Robert Millikan had precisely measured all three anomalous behaviors. The data was unambiguous.
beats.tts_normalized_text: By 1902, Philipp Lenard had identified the ejected particles as electrons. By 1914, Robert Millikan had precisely measured all three anomalous behaviors. The data was unambiguous.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Lenard (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Philipp Lenard at apparatus, or a cathode-ray-era laboratory, circa 1900

beats.narration_text: Failure one: threshold frequency. Below a material-specific cutoff, no electrons are ejected — regardless of light intensity. A blinding red lamp: nothing. A dim UV lamp: immediate ejection.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Failure one: threshold frequency. Below a material-specific cutoff, no electrons are ejected — regardless of light intensity. A blinding red lamp: nothing. A dim UV lamp: immediate ejection.

shot.note: AI-generated plate — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO+ANNOTATION] carbon arc lamp / searchlight plate; annotation plane adds 'RED: nothing' vs 'UV: instantly' chips at assembly — plate slots now

beats.narration_text: Failure two: kinetic energy is independent of intensity. More light produces more electrons — but each electron has the same energy as before. Wave model predicts KE should scale with intensity. It does not.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Failure two: kinetic energy is independent of intensity. More light produces more electrons — but each electron has the same energy as before. Wave model predicts KE should scale with intensity. It does not.

viz.data: isotype: one row of electron squares, then double brightness -> two rows, every square the SAME size; 'same energy each' chip
viz.note: KE independent of intensity (chapter fact two)

beats.narration_text: Failure three: instantaneous emission. Electrons are ejected within nanoseconds at any intensity above threshold. The wave model requires time to accumulate enough energy. That delay is never observed.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Failure three: instantaneous emission. Electrons are ejected within nanoseconds at any intensity above threshold. The wave model requires time to accumulate enough energy. That delay is never observed.

shot.note: AI-generated plate — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] gold-leaf electroscope or precision lab instrument, circa 1900; set shot.focus on the instrument

beats.narration_text: Why the wave model fails: waves distribute energy continuously across a surface. They cannot deliver a concentrated threshold dose to one electron. Every wave-based prediction is wrong in the same systematic direction.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Why the wave model fails: waves distribute energy continuously across a surface. They cannot deliver a concentrated threshold dose to one electron. Every wave-based prediction is wrong in the same systematic direction.

viz.data: three classical expectations as serif labels — 'any color, given time' / 'brighter means faster' / 'energy needs to accumulate' — each gets a hand-drawn terracotta strike as the narration lands
viz.note: one editor's-pen voice per graphic: the strikes ARE the voice here

beats.narration_text: In 1905, Albert Einstein proposed the solution. Working as a patent clerk in Bern, he applied Planck's energy quantization — which Planck had limited to emission — to light itself.
beats.tts_normalized_text: In 1905, Albert Einstein proposed the solution. Working as a patent clerk in Bern, he applied Planck's energy quantization — which Planck had limited to emission — to light itself.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Einstein (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Einstein at the Bern patent office era (~1905, young, seated at desk); set shot.focus on his face

beats.narration_text: The photon hypothesis: light energy is quantized into discrete packets. Each packet carries energy proportional to frequency. This is not a wave spreading energy — it is a particle delivering a fixed dose.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The photon hypothesis: light energy is quantized into discrete packets. Each packet carries energy proportional to frequency. This is not a wave spreading energy — it is a particle delivering a fixed dose.

viz.data: a smooth navy wave greys out; a stream of discrete terracotta dots (photons) crosses in its place; 'Lichtquanten' serif label with hairline
viz.note: Einstein 1905: quantizes the FIELD itself — the step beyond Planck (chapter)

beats.narration_text: The mechanism: one photon interacts with one electron. The photon is absorbed completely or not at all — no partial transfers. This is the operational rule that resolves all three failures.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The mechanism: one photon interacts with one electron. The photon is absorbed completely or not at all — no partial transfers. This is the operational rule that resolves all three failures.

viz.data: one terracotta photon dot arrives at a slate metal edge, one navy electron leaves; a second sub-threshold photon fades with an X — whole packet or nothing

beats.narration_text: The equation: K-max equals h-nu minus phi. Maximum electron kinetic energy equals photon energy minus work function. This single expression predicts the threshold, the KE-frequency relationship, and the instant emission.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The equation: Kay-max equals H-new minus fie. Maximum electron kinetic energy equals photon energy minus work function. This single expression predicts the threshold, the KE-frequency relationship, and the instant emission.

tangent.rhs: The photon's payment, minus the metal's exit fee.
tangent.claim: Exactly this — each photon acts alone, whole or not at all.

example.scenario: UV at 300 nm on sodium. Fee: 2.28 eV.
example.verdict: UV: electron keeps 1.85 eV · green: 0.01 short — nothing
example.cost: Sub-threshold photons never add up. Each acts alone.

tangent.reentry: And that is the whole law. Back to the three facts.

beats.narration_text: Variable definitions. K-max: maximum electron kinetic energy in electron volts. h-nu: photon energy — h is Planck's constant, nu is frequency. Phi: work function, a material property in electron volts. Sodium work function: 2.28 eV.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Variable definitions. Kay-max: maximum electron kinetic energy in electron volts. H-new: photon energy — h is Planck's constant, new is frequency. Fie: work function, a material property in electron volts. Sodium work function: 2.28 electron volts.

beats.narration_text: Worked values: UV at 300 nm on sodium — photon energy 4.13 eV, work function 2.28 eV, K-max 1.85 eV, ejection occurs. Green at 546 nm — photon energy 2.27 eV, short by 0.01 eV, no ejection. The threshold is binary and exact.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Worked values: UV at 300 nanometers on sodium — photon energy 4.13 electron volts, work function 2.28 electron volts, K-max 1.85 electron volts, ejection occurs. Green at 546 nanometers — photon energy 2.27 electron volts, short by 0.01 electron volts, no ejection. The threshold is binary and exact.

beats.narration_text: The three failures resolved. Threshold: photon energy must exceed work function — no other condition. KE from frequency not intensity: each photon carries a fixed packet set by frequency alone. Instant emission: no accumulation required — one photon, one electron, one transaction.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The three failures resolved. Threshold: photon energy must exceed work function — no other condition. KE from frequency not intensity: each photon carries a fixed packet set by frequency alone. Instant emission: no accumulation required — one photon, one electron, one transaction.

viz.data: the three impossible facts return as serif labels, each now getting a navy check on its narration cue — the mirror of B07's strikes

beats.narration_text: Millikan attempted to disprove Einstein's photon hypothesis — two years of precise measurements on freshly prepared metal surfaces in vacuum.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Millikan attempted to disprove Einstein's photon hypothesis — two years of precise measurements on freshly prepared metal surfaces in vacuum.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Millikan (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Robert Millikan portrait or at apparatus (Caltech-era acceptable); set shot.focus on his face

beats.narration_text: Result: every metal produced the same slope h over e in the stopping-potential vs frequency plot. Einstein's equation fit all data. Millikan published the confirmation he had designed to refute — his phrase: "in spite of my personal conviction."
beats.tts_normalized_text: Result: every metal produced the same slope h over e in the stopping-potential vs frequency plot. Einstein's equation fit all data. Millikan published the confirmation he had designed to refute — his phrase: "in spite of my personal conviction."

viz.data: axes: stopping potential vs frequency; three parallel navy lines (sodium, lithium, potassium) draw on with different thresholds, one crimson slope label 'h/e — the same for every metal'

beats.narration_text: Einstein received the 1921 Nobel for this specifically — not for relativity. Millikan's 1923 Nobel followed for the measurements that confirmed it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Einstein received the 1921 Nobel for this specifically — not for relativity. Millikan's 1923 Nobel followed for the measurements that confirmed it.

viz.data: two slate cards: EINSTEIN · 1921 · 'for the photoelectric effect' / MILLIKAN · 1923 · 'for measuring h' — terracotta ring on 'not relativity' line
viz.note: Einstein Nobel 1921 for photoelectric effect; Millikan 1923 (chapter)

beats.narration_text: Use this model for: threshold calculations, UV sensor design, classical EM failure analysis. Do not use for: coherent or pulsed laser sources, multiphoton ionization, or near-threshold nonlinear regimes.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Use this model for: threshold calculations, UV sensor design, classical EM failure analysis. Do not use for: coherent or pulsed laser sources, multiphoton ionization, or near-threshold nonlinear regimes.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Einstein (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Einstein portrait ~1905 (young, not the elderly icon); name label + dates at assembly; bio kicker per SKILL.md

beats.narration_text: Here is what this looks like in a real session. Run this in Claude to build a working photoelectric threshold simulation for any metal. Change the metal, change the wavelength — the table shows exactly where emission starts and stops.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Here is what this looks like in a real session. Run this in Claude to build a working photoelectric threshold simulation for any metal. Change the metal, change the wavelength — the table shows exactly where emission starts and stops.

cli_exercise.ask: claude "Write a Python simulation of the photoelectric effect for sodium (work function 2.28 eV) and copper (4.65 eV). For each metal, sweep light frequency from 4e14 Hz to 2e15 Hz in 20 steps. Output a table: frequency (Hz), wavelength (nm), photon energy (eV), max electron KE for sodium (eV), max electron KE for copper (eV). Mark NO EMISSION where KE would be negative."
cli_exercise.output_description: A 20-row table comparing sodium and copper. Sodium threshold appears around 549 nm; copper requires deep UV at 267 nm. Rows below each threshold are marked NO EMISSION.
cli_exercise.change: Add gold (work function 5.10 eV) as a third column and re-run.
cli_exercise.output2_description: Same table with a gold column added. Gold requires deep UV below 243 nm; all visible and near-UV rows show NO EMISSION for gold.
cli_exercise.next_step: Run it with your own metal — look up its work function on NIST and substitute it into the simulation.

props.command: Write a Python simulation of the photoelectric effect for sodium (2.28 eV) and copper (4.65 eV). Sweep frequency, output threshold table, mark NO EMISSION.
